source.title: 2018 AHA/ACC/AACVPR/AAPA/ABC/ACPM/ADA/AGS/APhA/ASPC/NLA/PCNA Guideline on the Management of Blood Cholesterol
source.organization: Journal of the American College of Cardiology

extraction.summary: The 2018 AHA/ACC guideline provides evidence-based recommendations for cholesterol management to reduce atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease (ASCVD) risk. The guideline emphasizes heart-healthy lifestyle across all age groups and uses a risk-stratified approach to statin therapy with specific LDL-C targets based on patient risk categories.

population.age_criteria: Adults ≥20 years of age, with specific focus on ages 40-75 for primary prevention
population.risk_criteria: Based on ASCVD risk categories: clinical ASCVD, severe hypercholesterolemia (LDL-C ≥190 mg/dL), diabetes mellitus, and primary prevention with 10-year ASCVD risk calculation
population.disease_definition: ASCVD includes acute coronary syndrome, MI history, stable/unstable angina, coronary/arterial revascularization, stroke, TIA, or peripheral artery disease of atherosclerotic origin
population.exclusions: Pregnancy, childhood (for most recommendations), active liver disease, severe kidney disease (for some medications)
population.severity_staging: Very high-risk ASCVD (multiple major events or 1 major event + multiple high-risk conditions), high-risk (single ASCVD event), moderate-risk (10-year risk 7.5-19.9%), low-risk (<7.5%)

key_thresholds.context: Threshold for adding non-statin therapy in very high-risk ASCVD
Threshold for moderate-intensity statin in primary prevention
Indicates statin therapy unless otherwise deferred
Threshold for adding PCSK9 inhibitor in very high-risk patients
Alternative threshold for PCSK9 inhibitor consideration
Target percentage reduction with statin therapy
Kidney function threshold for diabetes risk enhancement
Optimal LDL-C reduction for maximum ASCVD risk reduction
Borderline 10-year risk range for primary prevention
Identification threshold for children and adolescents
Children/adolescents with family history of early atherosclerosis
Risk threshold for severe hypertriglyceridemia in pregnancy
Zero score indicates very low near-term risk

recommendations.recommendation: Emphasize heart-healthy lifestyle across all age groups as foundation of ASCVD risk reduction
In clinical ASCVD patients, use high-intensity statin therapy or maximally tolerated statin to reduce LDL-C by ≥50%
recommendations.notes: Greater LDL-C reduction provides greater risk reduction

recommendations.recommendation: In very high-risk ASCVD with LDL-C ≥70 mg/dL on maximal statin, add ezetimibe
recommendations.notes: Consider PCSK9 inhibitor if still ≥70 mg/dL on statin+ezetimibe

recommendations.recommendation: Adults 40-75 years with diabetes and LDL-C ≥70 mg/dL: start moderate-intensity statin without risk calculation
recommendations.notes: Consider high-intensity statin for higher-risk patients

recommendations.recommendation: Primary prevention ages 40-75: have clinician-patient risk discussion before starting statins
recommendations.notes: Include major risk factors, risk-enhancing factors, benefits/risks

recommendations.recommendation: High-intensity statin therapy in patients ≤75 years with clinical ASCVD
Moderate-intensity statin when high-intensity contraindicated or not tolerated
Maximally tolerated statin plus ezetimibe before PCSK9 inhibitor
recommendations.notes: For very high-risk patients considered for PCSK9

recommendations.recommendation: PCSK9 inhibitor for very high-risk patients with LDL-C ≥70 mg/dL on maximal therapy
recommendations.notes: Following clinician-patient discussion about benefit, safety, cost

recommendations.recommendation: Ezetimibe addition for very high-risk patients with LDL-C ≥70 mg/dL
High-intensity statin therapy for clinical ASCVD patients
recommendations.notes: Primary recommendation based on CTT meta-analysis

recommendations.recommendation: Add ezetimibe to maximally tolerated statin in very high-risk ASCVD patients with LDL-C ≥70 mg/dL
Add PCSK9 inhibitor in very high-risk ASCVD patients with LDL-C ≥70 mg/dL on maximally tolerated statin
recommendations.notes: After clinician-patient discussion of net benefit, safety, and cost

recommendations.recommendation: Maximally tolerated statin therapy for severe hypercholesterolemia (LDL-C ≥190 mg/dL)
Consider moderate or high-intensity statin in patients >75 years with ASCVD
recommendations.notes: After evaluating potential benefits vs adverse effects and patient frailty

recommendations.recommendation: Moderate-intensity statin therapy in adults 40-75 years with diabetes mellitus
recommendations.notes: Indicated regardless of estimated 10-year ASCVD risk

recommendations.recommendation: High-intensity statin therapy for diabetes patients with multiple ASCVD risk factors
recommendations.notes: Aim to reduce LDL-C by 50% or more

recommendations.recommendation: Continue statin therapy in adults >75 years with diabetes already on treatment
Consider ezetimibe addition for diabetes patients with ≥20% 10-year ASCVD risk
Consider PCSK9 inhibitor for FH patients with persistently elevated LDL-C
recommendations.notes: After clinician-patient discussion of benefits vs costs

recommendations.recommendation: In adults at intermediate-risk, statin therapy reduces risk of ASCVD, and in the context of a risk discussion, if a decision is made for statin therapy, a moderate-intensity statin should be recommended
In intermediate-risk patients, LDL-C levels should be reduced by 30% or more, and for optimal ASCVD risk reduction, especially in high-risk patients, levels should be reduced by 50% or more
Clinicians and patients should engage in a risk discussion that considers risk factors, adherence to healthy lifestyle, potential for ASCVD risk-reduction benefits, and potential for adverse effects
In intermediate-risk adults, risk-enhancing factors favor initiation or intensification of statin therapy

In intermediate-risk or selected borderline-risk adults, if the decision about statin use remains uncertain, it is reasonable to use a CAC score in the decision to withhold, postpone or initiate statin therapy
Measure fasting lipids 4-12 weeks after statin initiation and every 3-12 months thereafter
In adults >75 years, moderate-intensity statin may be reasonable for LDL-C 70-189 mg/dL
Intensify lifestyle therapy in children/adolescents with obesity-related lipid disorders
recommendations.notes: Include caloric restriction and aerobic activity

recommendations.recommendation: Consider statin therapy in children ≥10 years with LDL-C ≥190 mg/dL after lifestyle therapy
recommendations.notes: If inadequate response to 3-6 months lifestyle therapy

recommendations.recommendation: Intensified lifestyle modification therapy for children and adolescents with lipid abnormalities and obesity including moderate caloric restriction and 30-60 minutes of moderate to vigorous activity on most days
recommendations.notes: Over and above usual therapy for childhood obesity

recommendations.recommendation: Consider statin therapy in children ≥10 years with FH who have not responded to 3-6 months of lifestyle therapy
recommendations.notes: May be considered at age 8 years with concerning family history or extremely elevated LDL-C

recommendations.recommendation: Address and treat lifestyle factors, secondary factors, and medications that increase triglycerides in adults with moderate hypertriglyceridemia
recommendations.notes: Include obesity, metabolic syndrome, diabetes, liver/kidney disease

recommendations.recommendation: Consider pregnancy-related conditions and premature menopause when discussing statin therapy benefit in women
recommendations.notes: Include preeclampsia, gestational diabetes, preterm delivery

recommendations.recommendation: In adults 40-75 years with LDL-C 70-189 mg/dL and 10-year ASCVD risk ≥7.5%, CKD not treated with dialysis is a risk-enhancing factor and initiation of moderate-intensity statin or moderate-intensity statin combined with ezetimibe can be useful
In adults with advanced kidney disease requiring dialysis who are currently on LDL-lowering therapy with a statin, it may be reasonable to continue the statin
In adults with advanced kidney disease requiring dialysis treatment, initiation of a statin is not recommended
recommendations.notes: Based on lack of benefit in RCTs

recommendations.recommendation: A clinician-patient risk discussion is recommended before initiation of statin therapy to review net clinical benefit
In patients with increased diabetes mellitus risk or new-onset diabetes mellitus, it is recommended to continue statin therapy with added emphasis on lifestyle modifications
Use reassess, rediscuss, and rechallenge strategy for SAMS management with dose reduction or alternative agents
recommendations.notes: Majority of patients can be successfully treated with at least one statin

recommendations.recommendation: Interventions focused on improving adherence to prescribed therapy including telephone reminders, calendar reminders, and pharmacist-led interventions
Patient-clinician discussion should take place to promote shared decision-making including potential benefits, adverse effects, and patient preferences
recommendations.notes: Essential for successful therapy initiation and continuation

recommendations.recommendation: Health systems should identify patients not receiving guideline-directed therapy and facilitate initiation using multifaceted strategies
High-intensity statin therapy for secondary ASCVD prevention
recommendations.notes: Based on multiple large randomized controlled trials showing cardiovascular benefit

recommendations.recommendation: Statin therapy for primary prevention in patients with LDL-C ≥190 mg/dL
Addition of ezetimibe to statin therapy in high-risk patients with inadequate LDL-C reduction
PCSK9 inhibitors for very high-risk patients with inadequate response to maximally tolerated statin plus ezetimibe
High-intensity statin therapy for patients with severe hypercholesterolemia (LDL-C ≥190 mg/dL)
Moderate to high-intensity statin therapy for adults with diabetes mellitus
Consider coronary artery calcium (CAC) scoring for risk assessment in intermediate risk patients
Use pooled cohort risk equations for 10-year ASCVD risk assessment in adults 40-75 years
recommendations.notes: Foundation for statin therapy decisions in primary prevention

recommendations.recommendation: Consider coronary artery calcium testing for risk refinement when treatment decision is uncertain
Initiate statin therapy based on risk assessment and patient-clinician discussion
Monitor lipid response to therapy with follow-up testing

medications.indication: Clinical ASCVD, severe hypercholesterolemia, high-risk diabetes
Add-on therapy when LDL-C goals not met with statin
Very high-risk ASCVD or severe hypercholesterolemia with inadequate LDL-C lowering
Very high-risk patients with LDL-C ≥70 mg/dL on maximally tolerated therapy
May consider in severe hypercholesterolemia with triglycerides ≤300 mg/dL
Primary prevention in diabetes mellitus 40-75 years
Diabetes with multiple risk factors, severe hypercholesterolemia
Add-on therapy or alternative when statins not tolerated
Children ≥10 years with FH after lifestyle therapy failure
CKD patients with intermediate ASCVD risk

medications.dose: 140 mg every 2 weeks or 420 mg monthly

red_flags: Unexplained muscle pain, tenderness, or weakness on statin therapy
Significant elevation in liver enzymes (>3x upper limit normal)
New onset diabetes in high-risk patients
Rhabdomyolysis symptoms
Multiple major ASCVD events in short timeframe
Family history of premature ASCVD with very high cholesterol levels
Severe hypertriglyceridemia when fasting triglycerides ≥300 mg/dL with bile acid sequestrants
Statin-associated muscle symptoms requiring dose adjustment or discontinuation
Drug-drug interactions with statins requiring monitoring
LDL-C levels consistently below 25 mg/dL on PCSK9 inhibitor therapy
Multiple major ASCVD events in young patients
Severe adverse effects from statin therapy requiring discontinuation
Baseline LDL-C ≥220 mg/dL with inadequate response to maximally tolerated therapy
LDL-C levels ≥220 mg/dL with non-treatment level ≥130 mg/dL despite maximally tolerated statin and ezetimibe
Diabetes mellitus with long duration (≥10 years type 2, ≥20 years type 1) in young adults 20-39 years
Presence of diabetic microvascular complications (retinopathy, neuropathy, nephropathy)
Ankle-brachial index <0.9 in diabetes patients
Family history of premature ASCVD with high LDL-C levels
Persistent cigarette smoking with diabetes mellitus
Persistent cigarette smoking with CAC score of zero
Diabetes mellitus with CAC score of zero after 5 years
Strong family history of premature ASCVD with CAC score of zero
Chronic inflammatory conditions (HIV, rheumatoid arthritis, psoriasis) with CAC score of zero
High 10-year ASCVD risk (≥20%) despite CAC score of zero
Functional decline in older adults
Multimorbidity limiting benefit potential
Frailty or reduced life expectancy
Persistent smoking despite treatment
Family history of premature ASCVD with high lifetime risk
Severe hypercholesterolemia (LDL-C ≥190 mg/dL) in young patients
Triglycerides ≥1,000 mg/dL (risk of acute pancreatitis)
Severe hypercholesterolemia (LDL-C ≥190 mg/dL) in children
Pregnancy in women on statin therapy
Family history of early cardiovascular disease with elevated cholesterol
Concerning family history with extremely elevated LDL-C in children age 8 years
Objective muscle weakness with statin use
Creatine kinase >10 times upper limit of normal with renal injury (rhabdomyolysis)
Severe statin-associated muscle symptoms
Signs or symptoms suggesting hepatotoxicity
Triglyceride levels ≥500 mg/dL at onset of pregnancy
Severe muscle weakness with statin use
Rhabdomyolysis
Persistent marked CK elevation
Statin-associated autoimmune myopathy with HMGCR antibodies and necrotizing myopathy
Statin-associated muscle symptoms with creatine kinase elevation >10 times upper limit of normal
New onset diabetes in patients on high-intensity statin therapy requiring monitoring
Drug interactions with statins that may increase adverse event risk
Cognitive impairment concerns in elderly patients on statin therapy
Acute coronary syndrome
New onset or worsening peripheral artery disease
Diabetic retinopathy with cardiovascular risk
Familial hypercholesterolemia with early ASCVD
Heart failure with reduced ejection fraction
Severe hypercholesterolemia requiring specialized management
Acute cardiovascular events during treatment
Significant muscle symptoms with elevated CK
New-onset diabetes in high-risk patients
Liver enzyme elevation >3x upper limit of normal
extraction.follow_up: Assess adherence and LDL-C response 4-12 weeks after statin initiation or dose adjustment, then repeat every 3-12 months as needed. Monitor for adverse effects and adjust therapy based on percentage LDL-C reduction from baseline.
extraction.escalation_path: Escalate care for: 1) Inability to achieve LDL-C goals despite maximally tolerated therapy, 2) Suspected familial hypercholesterolemia, 3) Recurrent ASCVD events on optimal therapy, 4) Statin intolerance requiring alternative approaches, 5) Complex cases requiring lipid specialist consultation
